subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Bachelor’s degree, likely in Education or a science-related field
Completed coursework in pedagogy, curriculum development, and adolescent psychology
Has participated in multiple professional development trainings since entering the field
Attends annual or semi-annual workshops focused on latest teaching methods and technology

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Works as a Postsecondary Education Administrator with a focus on classroom instruction and curriculum implementation
Executes lesson plans across high school subjects, including biology, chemistry, and career exploration
Handles both short-term and long-term teaching assignments as needed
Collaborates with other teachers and staff on curriculum review and best practices

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Employed in the education sector, specifically serving secondary/high school level
Operates within public or private school systems, depending on assignment
Works directly within academic administration and classroom instruction

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Works in a medium-to-large high school setting with several hundred to over a thousand students
Environment is collaborative, involving frequent interaction with teaching staff and administrative personnel
Facilities typically include science labs, classrooms, and shared technology resources

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Part of the K-12 education market segment, focusing on secondary education
Engages with both general education and STEM-focused curriculum
Supports a diverse student population with varying academic needs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 3-5 years of experience in current or similar educational roles
Began working in education administration and classroom teaching in late 20s
Experience includes both direct instruction and administrative collaboration

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Occupies an early to mid-career role, not yet at the senior or lead administrator level
Often works under the guidance of department heads or senior administrators
Acts as a peer collaborator with other teachers and staff

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Plans and executes lesson plans for high school students
Takes daily attendance and manages classroom logistics
Uses AI tools to brainstorm content ideas and ensure alignment with state standards
Provides classroom management using a trauma-informed approach

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends approximately 60-70% of time on direct classroom instruction and student engagement
Allocates 15-20% of time to lesson planning and material preparation
Dedicates 10% of time to administrative tasks such as submitting attendance and grading
Spends 5-10% collaborating with colleagues and attending professional development

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Has moderate autonomy in adapting or supplementing lesson plans provided by the department
Can independently select teaching methods and classroom management strategies
Major curricular changes or policy decisions require departmental approval
Responsible for real-time decisions regarding classroom issues and student needs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Engaging students in classroom discussions comes naturally
Taking attendance and managing basic classroom logistics are routine
Creating visual materials using Canva is straightforward and enjoyable
Utilizing Google Docs and Google Classroom for material distribution is intuitive

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Differentiating instruction for students with diverse learning needs is challenging
Developing lesson plans from scratch for unfamiliar subjects requires significant effort
Integrating new educational technologies or platforms can be complex initially
Managing large classes with behavioral challenges tests classroom management skills

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Grading short quizzes and diagnostic assessments is repetitive and often automated
Taking and submitting daily attendance is a routine, data-heavy task
Compiling weekly lesson materials and organizing folders can be streamlined with automation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys interactive activities like Kahoot! and group discussions with students
Finds brainstorming lesson ideas with AI tools both engaging and productive
Considers administrative paperwork and repetitive grading to be tedious
Prefers creative tasks such as designing visuals over routine data entry

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Switching between multiple digital platforms can cause workflow disruptions
Occasional internet connectivity issues impact access to online resources
Manual entry of grades or attendance is time-consuming
Collaboration with colleagues is sometimes slowed by inconsistent technology adoption

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Feels most productive and creative when working on tasks that are enjoyable, such as lesson design
Skill level is highest in areas that are also most enjoyable, like using digital tools for classroom management
Tedious tasks lead to lower engagement and slower completion rates
Learning new tools that improve workflow increases both enjoyment and productivity

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Has a positive attitude towards adopting new technologies that improve teaching effectiveness
Views tech learning as essential for staying current in education
Enjoys experimenting with new software and features
Sees technology as a way to make classroom management and lesson planning easier

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Considers themselves adaptable to new teaching platforms and tools
Usually requires minimal time to get comfortable with updates or changes
Learns most effectively through hands-on practice and peer demonstrations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Regularly participates in paid professional development and tech trainings
Willing to dedicate after-school hours to learning new tools if they are relevant
Prefers concise, practical training sessions to lengthy theoretical ones

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by the prospect of saving time and improving teaching quality
Workload can occasionally be a barrier to taking on additional tech training
Relevance to daily teaching tasks is a key factor in deciding to learn new tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers who share positive experiences with new tools encourage adoption
Departmental recommendations carry weight in decisions to try new technologies
Collaborative staff culture increases openness to tech experimentation

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses Google Docs and Google Drive daily for lesson planning and material sharing
Employs Google Classroom to post assignments and communicate with students
Utilizes ChatGPT and Claude for content brainstorming and logistics planning
Relies on QR code generators to provide student access to digital material

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Google Sheets is used to track work hours and ensure accurate payment
Canva is frequently used to design visuals and infographics for lessons
Kahoot! is used for creating interactive quizzes and student assessments

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Comfortable with core Google Workspace tools: Docs, Sheets, Slides, Classroom, and Drive
Has experience with QR code technology and online quiz platforms
Aware of and occasionally uses other EdTech resources as needed

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasionally faces issues integrating Google Classroom with non-Google apps
Switching files between different formats (e.g., PDF, Slides) can cause minor compatibility problems
QR codes sometimes fail to link correctly if folder permissions are not set properly

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands basic AI terms such as large language models, prompts, and outputs
Has a practical grasp of how LLMs generate text and provide suggestions
Keeps updated on AI trends through professional development and self-guided learning

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses ChatGPT (OpenAI) for lesson brainstorming and planning
Uses Claude (Anthropic) for generating examples and research avenues
Experiments with 'Pro' versions of AI models to compare features and performance

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Applies AI tools every week for lesson planning and logistics
Uses AI to adapt lesson content to specific state standards
Employs AI to draft letters of recommendation for students

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: Successfully used ChatGPT to generate creative warm-up activities that engaged students
Noticed that AI sometimes provides inaccurate or overly generic examples, requiring manual review
Found Claude helpful for brainstorming research projects but less effective for detailed grading rubrics
Learned to cross-check AI-generated content for alignment with curriculum and accuracy

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Participates in school-sponsored professional development sessions covering new EdTech, including AI
Peers occasionally share AI usage tips and prompt ideas during staff meetings
Relies on self-guided exploration for advanced AI features not covered by formal training

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers a collaborative approach, iteratively refining AI outputs for lesson planning
Often starts with a broad prompt and narrows down outputs in conversation with the model
Uses AI as a brainstorming partner rather than a one-time answer generator

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Treats AI models as assistants that provide starting points and inspiration
Retains final editorial control and reviews all AI-generated content before use
Sees AI as a supportive tool rather than a direct collaborator or replacement

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Open to experimenting with AI-driven workflows that save time and improve lesson quality
Willing to try new AI features if they integrate well with existing tools
Evaluates new workflows on their ability to enhance student engagement and learning

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces that allow for clarification and iteration
Finds natural language prompts more intuitive than rigid command syntax
Appreciates AI systems that can contextualize follow-up questions and build on previous responses

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Lesson planning and classroom management often draw on tacit knowledge of student needs and school culture
AI tools are used for inspiration but require human expertise for contextual adaptation
Grading and student feedback depend heavily on understanding individual learning profiles

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Generally trusts AI for brainstorming and generating draft materials, but not for final content
Always reviews AI outputs for accuracy and appropriateness before classroom use
Relies on human judgment for sensitive communications and critical feedback

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI to handle repetitive or time-consuming tasks like quiz generation
Believes humans should control final lesson content and student assessments
Sees greatest value in hybrid workflows combining AI efficiency with human oversight

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for administrative tasks (e.g., attendance, grading) if accuracy is ensured
Views automation of direct instruction or personalized feedback as potentially threatening to quality
Supports automation only when there is a clear mechanism for manual review and correction

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances the importance of critical thinking and adaptability
Sees some routine skills (e.g., drafting basic materials) as less crucial due to automation
Views interpersonal and classroom management skills as irreplaceable by AI

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Developing skills in prompt engineering to get optimal AI outputs
Taking responsibility for validating AI-generated content for alignment with standards
Learning to integrate AI tools into curriculum planning and student engagement strategies

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Mildly concerned about AI replacing repetitive administrative tasks
Does not feel threatened regarding core teaching or interpersonal responsibilities
Believes meaningful instruction and mentorship cannot be fully automated

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Always manually reviews AI-generated lesson materials before classroom use
School policies require human oversight for grading and critical feedback
Has the option to edit or reject any AI output as needed

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Notices a gradual increase in encouragement to experiment with AI tools
Sees more professional development sessions covering AI each year
Departmental discussions on responsible AI use are becoming more common

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI has the potential to significantly improve educational efficiency and personalization
Is optimistic about AI assisting educators but cautious about overreliance
Recognizes AI's potential to reduce administrative burdens across industries

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Believes ethical use of AI requires transparency and human oversight
Worries about potential bias in AI-generated materials if not carefully reviewed
Supports clear guidelines to prevent misuse or over-dependence on AI

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Would like more robust AI tools for differentiated instruction tailored to individual students
Desires AI integrations that can seamlessly connect all EdTech platforms used in schools
Sees value in AI tools that better support classroom management and student engagement analytics

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects routine administrative tasks to become increasingly automated
Foresees a greater emphasis on educators' roles as facilitators and mentors
Predicts increased demand for skills in AI tool evaluation and classroom integration

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Recommends ongoing, targeted training on AI tools for all staff
Supports creation of clear policies outlining acceptable AI use in curriculum and assessment
Advocates for regular review and updates of AI tools to ensure alignment with educational standards
